Lab 4: Spatial Predictive Analysis

Analyzing 311 Violations

Author

Dalanda Jalloh

Published

April 9, 2026

Assignment Overview

In this lab, you will apply the spatial predictive modeling techniques demonstrated in the class exercise using a different 311 service request type of your choice as the predictor variable. You will build a complete spatial predictive model, document your process, and interpret your results.

Learning Objectives

By completing this assignment, you will demonstrate your ability to:

  • Adapt example code to analyze a new dataset
  • Build spatial features for predictive modeling
  • Apply count regression techniques to spatial data
  • Implement spatial cross-validation
  • Interpret and communicate model results
  • Critically evaluate model performance

Step 1: Choose Your 311 Violation Type

Getting the Data

Visit the Chicago 311 Service Requests dataset:

https://data.cityofchicago.org/stories/s/311-Dataset-Changes-12-11-2018/d7nq-5g7t

Browse the available service request types (e.g., Graffiti Removal, Pothole Repair, Street Light Out, Sanitation Code Violations, etc.) and choose one violation type that interests you.

Requirements for Your Choice

  • Choose a different violation type than the abandoned cars we did in class.

Step 2: Complete the Analysis

Using the class exercise as your template, adapt the code to analyze your chosen 311 violation type.

What to Include

Work through all major sections of the analysis:

Part 0: Setup

# Load required packages
library(tidyverse)      # Data manipulation
library(sf)             # Spatial operations
library(here)           # Relative file paths
library(viridis)        # Color scales
library(terra)          # Raster operations (replaces 'raster')
library(spdep)          # Spatial dependence
library(FNN)            # Fast nearest neighbors
library(MASS)           # Negative binomial regression
library(patchwork)      # Plot composition (replaces grid/gridExtra)
library(knitr)          # Tables
library(kableExtra)     # Table formatting
library(classInt)       # Classification intervals
library(lubridate)
library(fixest)


# Spatstat for analyzing point patterns - split into sub-packages
library(spatstat.geom)    # Spatial geometries
library(spatstat.explore) # Spatial exploration/KDE

# Set options
options(scipen = 999)  # No scientific notation
set.seed(5080)         # Reproducibility, could be any number

# Create consistent theme for visualizations
theme_crime <- function(base_size = 11) {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size) +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = base_size + 1),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray30", size = base_size - 1),
      legend.position = "right",
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      axis.text = element_blank(),
      axis.title = element_blank()
    )
}

# Set as default
theme_set(theme_crime())

cat("✓ All packages loaded successfully!\n")
✓ All packages loaded successfully!
cat("✓ Working directory:", getwd(), "\n")
✓ Working directory: C:/Users/dalan/Desktop/PPA_CPLN5920/GitHub/CPLN5920_Jalloh_Portfolio_2/labs/lab_4

Part 1: Data Loading & Exploration

  • Load your 311 data and Chicago spatial boundaries
  • Create visualizations showing the spatial distribution of your violation type
  • Describe patterns you observe
#1.1 Load Chicago Spatial Data 

# Load police districts (used for spatial cross-validation)
policeDistricts <- 
  st_read("https://data.cityofchicago.org/api/geospatial/24zt-jpfn?method=export&format=GeoJSON") %>%
  st_transform('ESRI:102271') %>%
  dplyr::select(District = dist_num)
Reading layer `OGRGeoJSON' from data source 
  `https://data.cityofchicago.org/api/geospatial/24zt-jpfn?method=export&format=GeoJSON' 
  using driver `GeoJSON'

Dimension:     XY

Geodetic CRS:  WGS 84
# Load police beats (smaller administrative units)
policeBeats <- 
  st_read("https://data.cityofchicago.org/api/geospatial/n9it-hstw?method=export&format=GeoJSON") %>%
  st_transform('ESRI:102271') %>%
  dplyr::select(Beat = beat_num)

  `https://data.cityofchicago.org/api/geospatial/n9it-hstw?method=export&format=GeoJSON'

  st_transform('ESRI:102271')

Analysis: In step 1.1, I loaded the needed spatial data for the analysis which was easily accessible from the city of Chicago’s open data portal.

#1.2 Load Rodent Complaint Data 

rodent_data<- read.csv("data/raw/311_Service_Requests_-_Rodent_Baiting_-_Historical_20260411.csv")

rodent_data$Creation.Date <- mdy(rodent_data$Creation.Date)

rodent_data_filter <- rodent_data %>% 
  filter(between(Creation.Date,
                 as.Date("2018-01-01"),
                 as.Date("2018-12-31"))) %>% 
  filter(Status== "Completed")

rodent_data_filter<- rodent_data_filter %>% 
  filter(!is.na(Longitude), !is.na(Latitude))

rodent_data_filter <- st_as_sf(rodent_data_filter, coords = c("Longitude", "Latitude"), crs = 4326)
rodent_data_filter <- st_transform(rodent_data_filter, 'ESRI:102271')

glimpse(rodent_data_filter)

# Check the data
cat("\n✓ Loaded rodent data\n")

✓ Loaded rodent data
cat("  - Number of rodent complaints:", nrow(rodent_data_filter), "\n")
  - Number of rodent complaints: 37379

Analysis: In step 1.2, I filtered my data to only include complaints made in the most updated year of the data-set, 2018. I choose to analyze rodent complaints because cities are heavily burdened by the destruction that rats cause. They pose public health and ecological risks and damage infrastructure. If cities are able to predict where rats are likely to be appear they can plan for and minimize their damage.

#1.3 Visualize Point Data 
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 5

# Simple point map
p1 <- ggplot() + 
  geom_sf(data = chicago_boundary, fill = "gray95", color = "gray60") +
  geom_sf(data = rodent_data_filter, color = "#d62828", size = 0.1, alpha = 0.4) +
  labs(
    title = "Rodent Complaints",
    subtitle = paste0("Chicago 2018, n = ", nrow(rodent_data_filter))
  )

p2 <- ggplot() + 
  geom_sf(data = chicago_boundary, fill = "gray95", color = "gray60") +
  geom_density_2d_filled(
    data = data.frame(st_coordinates(rodent_data_filter)),
    aes(X, Y),
    alpha = 0.7,
    bins = 8
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(
    option = "plasma",
    direction = -1,
    guide = "none"  # Modern ggplot2 syntax (not guide = FALSE)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Density Surface",
    subtitle = "Kernel density estimation"
  )

# Combine plots using patchwork (modern approach)
p1 + p2 + 
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Spatial Distribution of Rodent Complaints in Chicago",
    tag_levels = 'A'
  )

Analysis: In step 1.3, I was able to map the spatial locations of the complaints and create a kernel density map which smoothed the points out to better see where they were concentrated within the context of the Chicago.

Part 2: Fishnet Grid Creation

  • Create a 500m x 500m fishnet grid
  • Aggregate your violations to grid cells
  • Visualize the count distribution
#2.1 Understanding the Fishnet 

# Create 500m x 500m grid
fishnet <- st_make_grid(
  chicago_boundary,
  cellsize = 500,  # 500 meters per cell
  square = TRUE
) %>%
  st_sf() %>%
  mutate(uniqueID = row_number()) %>%
  st_transform('ESRI:102271') 

# Keep only cells that intersect Chicago
fishnet <- fishnet[chicago_boundary, ]

# View basic info
cat("✓ Created fishnet grid\n")
✓ Created fishnet grid
cat("  - Number of cells:", nrow(fishnet), "\n")
  - Number of cells: 2458 
cat("  - Cell size:", 500, "x", 500, "meters\n")
  - Cell size: 500 x 500 meters
cat("  - Cell area:", round(st_area(fishnet[1,])), "square meters\n")
  - Cell area: 250000 square meters
#2.2 Aggregate Burglaries to Grid 

# Spatial join: which cell contains each rodent complaint?
rodent_fishnet <- st_join(rodent_data_filter, fishnet, join = st_within) %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  group_by(uniqueID) %>%
  summarize(countrodent_data_filter = n()) 

glimpse(rodent_fishnet)
Rows: 1,916
Columns: 2
$ uniqueID                <int> 38, 50, 91, 92, 102, 104, 144, 145, 155, 156, …
$ countrodent_data_filter <int> 1, 1, 2, 1, 1, 3, 1, 8, 1, 4, 2, 18, 7, 3, 1, …
st_crs(rodent_fishnet)
Coordinate Reference System: NA
# Join back to fishnet (cells with 0 burglaries will be NA)
fishnet <- fishnet %>%
  left_join(rodent_fishnet, by = "uniqueID") %>%
  mutate(countrodent_data_filter = replace_na(countrodent_data_filter, 0))

# Summary statistics
cat("\nrodents count distribution:\n")

rodents count distribution:
summary(fishnet$countrodent_datasf_proj)
Length  Class   Mode 
     0   NULL   NULL 
cat("\nCells with zero rodents:", 
    sum(fishnet$rodent_data_filter == 0), 
    "/", nrow(fishnet),
    "(", round(100 * sum(fishnet$countrodent_data_filter == 0) / nrow(fishnet), 1), "%)\n")

Cells with zero rodents: 0 / 2458 ( 22.1 %)

Analysis: In part 2, a fishnet grid was created for the city of Chicago. A fishnet grid places the point data (complaints) over a grid of cells that acts as the spatial unit needed to aggregate counts and do other spatial analyses. Because 15.8% of the grids had 0 complaints, it is likely that overdispersion (mean =/ variance) become an issue when running the regression later on.

Part 3: Spatial Features

  • Calculate k-nearest neighbor features
  • Perform Local Moran’s I analysis
  • Identify hot spots and cold spots
  • Create distance-to-hotspot measures
  • Join any additional contextual data if you are looking for more to do and really get into this (e.g., demographics, land use)
#3.1 Create a Kernel Density Baseline 

# Convert burglaries to ppp (point pattern) format for spatstat
rodents_ppp <- as.ppp(
  st_coordinates(rodent_data_filter),
  W = as.owin(st_bbox(chicago_boundary))
)

# Calculate KDE with 1km bandwidth
kde_rodents <- density.ppp(
  rodents_ppp,
  sigma = 1000,  # 1km bandwidth
  edge = TRUE    # Edge correction
)

# Convert to terra raster (modern approach, not raster::raster)
kde_raster <- rast(kde_rodents)

# Extract KDE values to fishnet cells
fishnet <- fishnet %>%
  mutate(
    kde_value = terra::extract(
      kde_raster,
      vect(fishnet),
      fun = mean,
      na.rm = TRUE
    )[, 2]  # Extract just the values column
  )

cat("✓ Calculated KDE baseline\n")
✓ Calculated KDE baseline
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, aes(fill = kde_value), color = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = chicago_boundary, fill = NA, color = "white", linewidth = 1) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(
    name = "KDE Value",
    option = "plasma"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Kernel Density Estimation Baseline",
    subtitle = "Simple Spatial Smoothing of Rodent Complaint locations"
  ) +
  theme_crime()

Analysis: In step 3.1, a baseline density map is created as a benchmark to compare our model to. It works by converting the point into a raster overlay to better see where the points are concentrated within the city.

#3.2 Create Spatial Predictor Variable 

garbage_carts<- read.csv("data/raw/311_Service_Requests_-_Garbage_Carts_-_Historical_20260411.csv") %>% 
  filter(!is.na(Latitude), !is.na(Longitude)) %>%
  st_as_sf(coords = c("Longitude", "Latitude"), crs = 4326) %>%
  st_transform('ESRI:102271')

garbage_carts$Creation.Date <- mdy(garbage_carts$Creation.Date)

garbage_carts<- garbage_carts %>% 
  filter(between(Creation.Date,
                 as.Date("2018-01-01"),
                 as.Date("2018-12-31"))) %>% 
  filter(Status== "Completed")

# Aggregate garbage cart to fishnet
carts_fishnet <- st_join(garbage_carts, fishnet, join = st_within) %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  group_by(uniqueID) %>%
  summarize(carts = n())

# Join to fishnet
fishnet <- fishnet %>%
  left_join(carts_fishnet, by = "uniqueID") %>%
  mutate(carts = replace_na(carts, 0))

cat(" Cart distribution:\n")
 Cart distribution:
summary(fishnet$carts)
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
   0.00    2.00   12.00   14.27   23.00   66.00 
#visualize the data 

p1 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, aes(fill = carts), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Count", option = "plasma") +
  labs(title = "Garbage Carts") +
  theme_crime()

p2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, aes(fill = countrodent_data_filter), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Count", option = "plasma") +
  labs(title = "Rodent Complaints") +
  theme_crime()

p1 + p2 +
  plot_annotation(title = "Are garbage carts and rodent complaints correlated?")

Analysis: In step 3.2, I am using garbage cart locations as a spatial predictor that can hopefully be used to predict the occurrence of rodent complaints. After the data is downloaded, it’s joined to the fishnet data frame created in the previous step so that the points can be aggregated. Upon initial glance of the two maps side by side, there does not seem to be any correlation between where garbage carts are located and where complaints are being called from. There is some overlap in certain areas but overall not as much as I suspected when I originally choose garbage cart lcoations as a predictor.

#3.3 Nearest Neighbor Feature

# Calculate mean distance to 3 nearest garbage carts 
# (Do this OUTSIDE of mutate to avoid sf conflicts)

# Get coordinates
fishnet_coords <- st_coordinates(st_centroid(fishnet))
carts_coords <- st_coordinates(garbage_carts)

# Calculate k nearest neighbors and distances
nn_result <- get.knnx(carts_coords, fishnet_coords, k = 3)

# Add to fishnet
fishnet <- fishnet %>%
  mutate(
    carts.nn = rowMeans(nn_result$nn.dist)
  )

cat("✓ Calculated nearest neighbor distances\n")
✓ Calculated nearest neighbor distances
summary(fishnet$carts.nn)
    Min.  1st Qu.   Median     Mean  3rd Qu.     Max. 
   5.149   72.860  120.587  258.191  276.213 2321.007

Analysis: In step 3.3, K nearest neighbors is used here to measure the average distance to 3 garbage cart locations. This helps to capture a spillover effect- how likely are rodent complaints affected by nearby garbage trucks. Once the mean distance was calculated, this value was joined with the fishnet data set to represent the mean distance per grid cell.

#3.4 Distance to Hotspot 

calculate_local_morans_knn_safe <- function(data, variable, k = 5) {
  keep <- !is.na(data[[variable]]) & !st_is_empty(data)
  data_sub <- data[keep, ]
  
  pts <- st_point_on_surface(st_geometry(data_sub))
  coords <- st_coordinates(pts)
  
  knn <- knearneigh(coords, k = k)
  nb <- knn2nb(knn)
  lw <- nb2listw(nb, style = "W", zero.policy = TRUE)
  
  lm <- localmoran(data_sub[[variable]], lw, zero.policy = TRUE)
  mean_val <- mean(data_sub[[variable]], na.rm = TRUE)
  
  data$local_i <- NA_real_
  data$p_value <- NA_real_
  data$is_significant <- NA
  data$moran_class <- NA_character_
  
  data$local_i[keep] <- lm[, 1]
  data$p_value[keep] <- lm[, 5]
  data$is_significant[keep] <- data$p_value[keep] < 0.05
  
  vals <- data[[variable]]
  sig <- data$is_significant
  li <- data$local_i
  
  data$moran_class <- dplyr::case_when(
    !sig ~ "Not Significant",
    li > 0 & vals > mean_val ~ "High-High",
    li > 0 & vals <= mean_val ~ "Low-Low",
    li < 0 & vals > mean_val ~ "High-Low",
    li < 0 & vals <= mean_val ~ "Low-High",
    TRUE ~ "Not Significant"
  )
  
  data
}

# Apply to garbage carts 
fishnet <- calculate_local_morans_knn_safe(fishnet, "carts", k = 5)

# Visualize hot spots
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(
    data = fishnet, 
    aes(fill = moran_class), 
    color = NA
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "High-High" = "#d7191c",
      "High-Low" = "#fdae61",
      "Low-High" = "#abd9e9",
      "Low-Low" = "#2c7bb6",
      "Not Significant" = "gray90"
    ),
    name = "Cluster Type"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Local Moran's I: Garbage Carts Clusters",
    subtitle = "High-High = Hot spots of disorder"
  ) +
  theme_crime()
# Get centroids of "High-High" cells (hot spots)
hotspots <- fishnet %>%
  filter(moran_class == "High-High") %>%
  st_point_on_surface()

# Calculate distance from each cell to nearest hot spot
if (nrow(hotspots) > 0) {
  fishnet <- fishnet %>%
    mutate(
      dist_to_hotspot = as.numeric(
        st_distance(st_point_on_surface(fishnet), hotspots %>% st_union())
      )
    )
  
  cat("✓ Calculated distance to abandoned car hot spots\n")
  cat("  - Number of hot spot cells:", nrow(hotspots), "\n")
} else {
  fishnet <- fishnet %>%
    mutate(dist_to_hotspot = 0)
  cat("⚠ No significant hot spots found\n")
}
✓ Calculated distance to abandoned car hot spots
  - Number of hot spot cells: 317

Analysis: In step 3.4, local Moran’s I identifies clusters or hotpots of where garbage carts are located and whether the clustering is significant or not. Afterwards, the hotspots are defined such as high-high and low-low which represents where high values cluster together and where low values cluster together. Then each grid’s distance(from the centroid) to the nearest cluster is calculated. High-high clustering appears in the center of the city while low-low clustering appears more so on the eastern edges of the city.

Part 4: Count Regression Models

  • Fit Poisson regression
  • Fit Negative Binomial regression
  • Compare model fit (AIC)
#4.1 Join Police Districts for Cross Validation 

# Join district information to fishnet
fishnet <- st_join(
  fishnet,
  policeDistricts,
  join = st_within,
  left = TRUE
) 

fishnet<- fishnet %>% 
filter(!is.na(District))  # Remove cells outside districts

cat("✓ Joined police districts\n")
✓ Joined police districts
cat("  - Districts:", length(unique(fishnet$District)), "\n")
  - Districts: 22 
cat("  - Cells:", nrow(fishnet), "\n")
  - Cells: 1708 
#4.2 Poisson Regression 

fishnet_model <- fishnet %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  dplyr::transmute(
    uniqueID = uniqueID,
    District = District,
    countrodent_data_filter = as.numeric(countrodent_data_filter),
    carts = as.numeric(carts),
    carts.nn = as.numeric(carts.nn),
    dist_to_hotspot = as.numeric(dist_to_hotspot)
  ) %>%
  dplyr::filter(
    is.finite(countrodent_data_filter),
    is.finite(carts),
    is.finite(carts.nn),
    is.finite(dist_to_hotspot)
  )


cat("✓ Prepared modeling data\n")
✓ Prepared modeling data
cat("  - Observations:", nrow(fishnet_model), "\n")
  - Observations: 1708 
cat("  - Variables:", ncol(fishnet_model), "\n")
  - Variables: 6 
# Fit Poisson regression

model_poisson <- fepois(
  countrodent_data_filter ~ carts + carts.nn + dist_to_hotspot,
  data = fishnet_model
)

# Summary
summary(model_poisson)
Poisson estimation, Dep. Var.: countrodent_data_filter
Observations: 1,708
Standard-errors: IID 
                 Estimate Std. Error  z value  Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept)      2.961886 0.02282403 129.7705 < 2.2e-16 ***
carts            0.018350 0.00057224  32.0673 < 2.2e-16 ***
carts.nn        -0.004227 0.00009440 -44.7789 < 2.2e-16 ***
dist_to_hotspot  0.000045 0.00000379  12.0127 < 2.2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
Log-Likelihood: -14,876.8   Adj. Pseudo R2: 0.27371 
           BIC:  29,783.4     Squared Cor.: 0.222272

Analysis: The results of the poisson regression show that all the predictors used in the model are significant as indicated by the p value of less than .05. Both carts and distance to hotspot have a rate ratio slighty above 1. This indicates that when garbage carts increase by one unit, so do rodent complaints. As distance to nearby garbage carts increase by one unit, rodent complaints decrease. As distance from hotspots increase, rodent complaints only slightly increase and should be interpreted with caution.

#4.3 Checking for over-dispersion 

# Calculate dispersion parameter

# Pearson residuals
pearson_resid <- residuals(model_poisson, type = "pearson")

# Number of observations
n <- nobs(model_poisson)

# Number of parameters
k <- length(coef(model_poisson))

# Dispersion statistic
dispersion <- sum(pearson_resid^2, na.rm = TRUE) / (n - k)

dispersion
[1] 16.57746
cat("Dispersion parameter:", round(dispersion, 2), "\n")
Dispersion parameter: 16.58 
cat("Rule of thumb: >1.5 suggests overdispersion\n")
Rule of thumb: >1.5 suggests overdispersion
if (dispersion > 1.5) {
  cat("⚠ Overdispersion detected! Consider Negative Binomial model.\n")
} else {
  cat("✓ Dispersion looks okay for Poisson model.\n")
}
⚠ Overdispersion detected! Consider Negative Binomial model.

Analysis: Over-dispersion is likely happening because many grid cells have zero or low rodent complaints, while a few have very high counts. This creates a lot of variation in the data, making the variance larger than the mean, which means that the Poisson model is not appropriate.

#4.4 Negative binomial regression 

# Fit Negative Binomial model

model_nb <- fenegbin(
  countrodent_data_filter ~ carts + carts.nn + dist_to_hotspot,
  data = fishnet_model
)

summary(model_nb)
ML estimation, family = Negative Binomial
Dep. Var.: countrodent_data_filter
Observations: 1,708
Standard-errors: IID 
                 Estimate Std. Error   z value    Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept)      2.791544   0.088369  31.58979   < 2.2e-16 ***
carts            0.024224   0.002790   8.68273   < 2.2e-16 ***
carts.nn        -0.004302   0.000217 -19.86761   < 2.2e-16 ***
dist_to_hotspot  0.000079   0.000018   4.33816 0.000014368 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1
Over-dispersion parameter: theta = 1.026066  
Log-Likelihood: -6,151.2   Adj. Pseudo R2: 0.064134
           BIC: 12,332.1     Squared Cor.: 0.216214
# Compare AIC (lower is better)
cat("\nModel Comparison:\n")

Model Comparison:
cat("Poisson AIC:", round(AIC(model_poisson), 1), "\n")
Poisson AIC: 29761.6 
cat("Negative Binomial AIC:", round(AIC(model_nb), 1), "\n")
Negative Binomial AIC: 12310.4

Analysis: In step 4.4 we try to fit our data with a negative binomial model which is better at handling data with dispersion. The AIC shows the negative binomial model is a better fit as indicated by the smaller value of 12310 in comparison to Poisson’s value of 29761.6.

Part 5: Spatial Cross-Validation (2017)

  • Implement Leave-One-Group-Out cross-validation on 2017 data
  • Calculate and report error metrics (MAE, RMSE)
#5.1 Leave One Group Out Cross-Validation
#5.2 Run Metrics 

# Get unique districts
districts <- unique(fishnet_model$District)
cv_results <- tibble()

cat("Running LOGO Cross-Validation...\n")
Running LOGO Cross-Validation...
for (i in seq_along(districts)) {
  
  test_district <- districts[i]
  
  # Split data
  train_data <- fishnet_model %>% filter(District != test_district)
  test_data <- fishnet_model %>% filter(District == test_district)
  
  # Fit model on training data
   model_cv <- fenegbin(
    countrodent_data_filter ~ carts + carts.nn + dist_to_hotspot,
    data = train_data
  )
  
  # Predict on test data
  test_data <- test_data %>%
    mutate(
      prediction = predict(model_cv, newdata = test_data, type = "response")
    )
  
  # Calculate metrics
  mae <- mean(abs(test_data$countrodent_data_filter - test_data$prediction))
  rmse <- sqrt(mean((test_data$countrodent_data_filter - test_data$prediction)^2))
  
  # Store results
  cv_results <- bind_rows(
    cv_results,
    tibble(
      fold = i,
      test_district = test_district,
      n_test = nrow(test_data),
      mae = mae,
      rmse = rmse
    )
  )
  
  cat("  Fold", i, "/", length(districts), "- District", test_district, 
      "- MAE:", round(mae, 2), "\n")
}
  Fold 1 / 22 - District 5 - MAE: 9.09 
  Fold 2 / 22 - District 4 - MAE: 10.82 
  Fold 3 / 22 - District 22 - MAE: 15.19 
  Fold 4 / 22 - District 6 - MAE: 10.74 
  Fold 5 / 22 - District 8 - MAE: 12.09 
  Fold 6 / 22 - District 7 - MAE: 8.34 
  Fold 7 / 22 - District 3 - MAE: 8.71 
  Fold 8 / 22 - District 2 - MAE: 9.09 
  Fold 9 / 22 - District 9 - MAE: 10.27 
  Fold 10 / 22 - District 10 - MAE: 15.3 
  Fold 11 / 22 - District 1 - MAE: 8.43 
  Fold 12 / 22 - District 12 - MAE: 14 
  Fold 13 / 22 - District 15 - MAE: 11.33 
  Fold 14 / 22 - District 11 - MAE: 11.55 
  Fold 15 / 22 - District 18 - MAE: 14.86 
  Fold 16 / 22 - District 25 - MAE: 12.22 
  Fold 17 / 22 - District 14 - MAE: 26.06 
  Fold 18 / 22 - District 19 - MAE: 27.47 
  Fold 19 / 22 - District 16 - MAE: 15.6 
  Fold 20 / 22 - District 17 - MAE: 13.88 
  Fold 21 / 22 - District 20 - MAE: 15.56 
  Fold 22 / 22 - District 24 - MAE: 17.54 
# Overall results
cat("\n✓ Cross-Validation Complete\n")

✓ Cross-Validation Complete
cat("Mean MAE:", round(mean(cv_results$mae), 2), "\n")
Mean MAE: 13.55 
cat("Mean RMSE:", round(mean(cv_results$rmse), 2), "\n")
Mean RMSE: 18.55 
# Show results
cv_results %>%
  arrange(desc(mae)) %>%
  kable(
    digits = 2,
    caption = "LOGO CV Results by District"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))
LOGO CV Results by District
fold test_district n_test mae rmse
18 19 63 27.47 35.16
17 14 46 26.06 34.12
22 24 41 17.54 23.35
19 16 129 15.60 18.96
21 20 30 15.56 24.32
10 10 63 15.30 23.37
3 22 112 15.19 17.41
15 18 30 14.86 19.56
12 12 73 14.00 22.90
20 17 82 13.88 21.50
16 25 85 12.22 18.26
5 8 197 12.09 16.57
14 11 43 11.55 14.33
13 15 32 11.33 14.08
2 4 235 10.82 16.30
4 6 63 10.74 14.71
9 9 107 10.27 15.92
1 5 98 9.09 11.80
8 2 56 9.09 11.01
7 3 43 8.71 11.56
11 1 28 8.43 11.97
6 7 52 8.34 11.02

Analysis: After running the cross validation we see that on average, the model’s predictions differ from observed rodent complaint counts by about 14 complaints per grid cell. The RMSE of 18.55 (which is higher than the MAE of 13.55) indicates that the model has a harder time predicting larger errors. The errors do vary across the different districts as well with the lowest MAE being 8.34 and the highest being 27.47. This indicates that some districts are harder to predict than others.

Part 6: Model Evaluation

  • Compare to KDE baseline
#6.1 Generate Final Predictions
#6.2 Compare Model vs KDE Baseline 

# Fit final model on all data
final_model <- fenegbin(
  countrodent_data_filter ~ carts + carts.nn + dist_to_hotspot,
  data = fishnet_model
)

# Add predictions back to fishnet
fishnet <- fishnet %>%
  mutate(
    prediction_nb = predict(final_model, newdata = fishnet_model, type = "response")[
      match(uniqueID, fishnet_model$uniqueID)
    ]
  )

# Also add KDE predictions (normalize to same scale as counts)
kde_sum <- sum(fishnet$kde_value, na.rm = TRUE)
count_sum <- sum(fishnet$countrodent_data_filter, na.rm = TRUE)

fishnet <- fishnet %>%
  mutate(
    prediction_kde = (kde_value / kde_sum) * count_sum
  )

#compare models 

# Create three maps

p1 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, aes(fill = countrodent_data_filter), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Count", option = "plasma", limits= c(0,15)) + 
  labs(title = "Actual Rodent Complaints") +
  theme_crime()

p2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, aes(fill = prediction_nb), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Predicted", option = "plasma", limits= c(0,15)) + 
  labs(title = "Model Predictions (Neg. Bin)") +
  theme_crime()

p3 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, aes(fill = prediction_kde), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Predicted", option = "plasma", limits= c(0, 15)) + 
  labs(title = "KDE Baseline Predictions") +
  theme_crime()

p1 + p2 + p3 +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Actual vs. Predicted Burglaries",
    subtitle = "Does our complex model outperform simple KDE?"
  )
# Calculate performance metrics
comparison <- fishnet %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  filter(!is.na(prediction_nb), !is.na(prediction_kde)) %>%
  summarize(
    model_mae = mean(abs(countrodent_data_filter - prediction_nb)),
    model_rmse = sqrt(mean((countrodent_data_filter - prediction_nb)^2)),
    kde_mae = mean(abs(countrodent_data_filter - prediction_kde)),
    kde_rmse = sqrt(mean((countrodent_data_filter - prediction_kde)^2))
  )

comparison %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") %>%
  tidyr::separate(metric, into = c("approach", "metric"), sep = "_") %>%
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = metric, values_from = value) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    digits = 2,
    caption = "Model Performance Comparison"
  ) %>%
  kableExtra::kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))
Model Performance Comparison
approach mae rmse
model 12.36 17.94
kde 9.61 14.51

Analysis: After using the model on all of the data at once, we can compare it with our baseline KDE model and where rodent complaints actually occurred. There is a big discrepancy between where the complaints appear in the city of Chicago. The spatial model(negative binomial) in general seems to underpredict more than it over predicts, although some areas are more over predicted. This can be due to poor predicting power of the model which could have been strengthened with more predictors. The KDE baseline model underpredicts especially north of Chicago and seems to predict more accurately at the southern end. I would argue that my model does a better job at predicting the actual rodent complaints because captures local variation better in areas that the KDE baseline model did not.

#6.3 Where does the model work well?  

#look where the model does not predict well 

# Calculate errors
fishnet <- fishnet %>%
  mutate(
    error_nb = countrodent_data_filter - prediction_nb,
    error_kde = countrodent_data_filter - prediction_kde,
    abs_error_nb = abs(error_nb),
    abs_error_kde = abs(error_kde)
  )

# Map errors
p1 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, aes(fill = error_nb), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    name = "Error",
    low = "#2166ac", mid = "white", high = "#b2182b",
    midpoint = 0
  ) +
  labs(title = "Model Errors (Actual - Predicted)") +
  theme_crime()

p2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, aes(fill = abs_error_nb), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Abs. Error", option = "magma") +
  labs(title = "Absolute Model Errors") +
  theme_crime()

p1 + p2
# Create nice summary table
model_summary <- broom::tidy(final_model, exponentiate = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(
    across(where(is.numeric), ~round(., 3))
  )
#Final Model Summary 

model_summary %>%
  kable(
    caption = "Final Negative Binomial Model Coefficients (Exponentiated)",
    col.names = c("Variable", "Rate Ratio", "Std. Error", "Z", "P-Value")
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover")) %>%
  footnote(
    general = "Rate ratios > 1 indicate positive association with rodent counts."
  )
Final Negative Binomial Model Coefficients (Exponentiated)
Variable Rate Ratio Std. Error Z P-Value
(Intercept) 2.792 0.088 31.590 0
carts 0.024 0.003 8.683 0
carts.nn -0.004 0.000 -19.868 0
dist_to_hotspot 0.000 0.000 4.338 0
.theta 1.026 0.040 25.803 0
Note:
Rate ratios > 1 indicate positive association with rodent counts.

Analysis: From the mapped errors or residuals, we see that higher residuals are concentrated in the north of the city, meaning that my model had a harder time predicting rodent complaints in that area. Lower value high residuals are also concentrated in the middle portion of the city. The mapped residuals help to explain where there is over or under prediction. This corresponds with the observations from above where much of the over and under prediction occurred in the northern part of the city. This step is an important part of the analysis to visually see how the residuals of the model compare to the actual dependent variable(rodent complaints). The clustering of residuals in north Chicago suggest that there are some spatial relationships specifically in that part of the city that are not accounted for in my current model.
